raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"The sharp increase in patenting activity. Over the last 10 years, the number of published patent families in GenAI models has grown from less than 800 in 2014 to more than 14,000 in 2023. There has been a big surge in patent activity beginning in 2017, with an average annual growth of around 45% since then. This coincides with the introduction of transformers in 2017."},{"excerpt":"The number of scientific publications has risen even more over the same period, from only around 100 in 2014 to more than 34,000 in 2023. In 2023 particularly, there has been a strong increase in scientific publications. It is likely that the release of very successful and popular GenAI models and tools in 2022 (ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, LlaMA, etc.) has initiated a new wave of GenAI research."},{"excerpt":"Diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023."}]}

examples.excerpt: The sharp increase in patenting activity. Over the last 10 years, the number of published patent families in GenAI models has grown from less than 800 in 2014 to more than 14,000 in 2023. There has been a big surge in patent activity beginning in 2017, with an average annual growth of around 45% since then. This coincides with the introduction of transformers in 2017.

The number of scientific publications has risen even more over the same period, from only around 100 in 2014 to more than 34,000 in 2023. In 2023 particularly, there has been a strong increase in scientific publications. It is likely that the release of very successful and popular GenAI models and tools in 2022 (ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, LlaMA, etc.) has initiated a new wave of GenAI research.

Diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"Since the introduction of the transformer in 2017, the deep neural network architecture behind the Large Language Models that have become synonymous with GenAI, the number of GenAI patents has increased by over 800%."},{"excerpt":"In 2023 particularly, there has been a strong increase in scientific publications. It is likely that the release of very successful and popular GenAI models and tools in 2022 (ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, LlaMA, etc.) has initiated a new wave of GenAI research."},{"excerpt":"Since 2017, China has published more patent families in this field every year than all other countries combined."}]}

examples.excerpt: Since the introduction of the transformer in 2017, the deep neural network architecture behind the Large Language Models that have become synonymous with GenAI, the number of GenAI patents has increased by over 800%.

In 2023 particularly, there has been a strong increase in scientific publications. It is likely that the release of very successful and popular GenAI models and tools in 2022 (ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, LlaMA, etc.) has initiated a new wave of GenAI research.
Since 2017, China has published more patent families in this field every year than all other countries combined.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"However, since 2017 we can see that the GenAI share of all AI patents has been increasing (from 4.2% in 2017 to 6.1% in 2023) (Figure 11)."},{"excerpt":"Over the last 10 years, the number of published patent families in GenAI models has increased from less than 800 in 2014 to more than 14,000 in 2023. There has been a big surge in patent activity beginning in 2017, with an average annual growth of around 45% since then."},{"excerpt":"The number of scientific publications has risen even more over the same period, from only around 100 in 2014 to more than 34,000 in 2023."}]}

examples.excerpt: However, since 2017 we can see that the GenAI share of all AI patents has been increasing (from 4.2% in 2017 to 6.1% in 2023) (Figure 11).

Over the last 10 years, the number of published patent families in GenAI models has increased from less than 800 in 2014 to more than 14,000 in 2023. There has been a big surge in patent activity beginning in 2017, with an average annual growth of around 45% since then.
The number of scientific publications has risen even more over the same period, from only around 100 in 2014 to more than 34,000 in 2023.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"Titanium is a metal with high industrial and commercial interest."},{"excerpt":"China dominates the patent filings in processes for the production of titanium dioxide from titanium, with 73 percent of all patents in our dataset being filed with China as the priority country – that is, the country where the first patent of each patent family is filed (Figure 10)."},{"excerpt":"Key players in the extraction of titanium dioxide from ilmenite comprise an almost equal mix of both industrial and academic players and are mainly from China."}]}

examples.excerpt: Titanium is a metal with high industrial and commercial interest.

China dominates the patent filings in processes for the production of titanium dioxide from titanium, with 73 percent of all patents in our dataset being filed with China as the priority country – that is, the country where the first patent of each patent family is filed (Figure 10).
Key players in the extraction of titanium dioxide from ilmenite comprise an almost equal mix of both industrial and academic players and are mainly from China.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"Patent activity has increased quite steadily since 2012 (compound annual growth rate, CAGR 2014–2019 = +8 percent) (Figure 7), indicating an increasing interest in the topic."},{"excerpt":"Between 2002 and 2022, there have been 459 patent families that describe the production of titanium dioxide from ilmenite, and this number is growing rapidly."},{"excerpt":"Patent activity on titanium metal production has remained quite steady since 2002, indicating a constant but not significantly growing interest in the topic (Figure 16)."}]}

examples.excerpt: Patent activity has increased quite steadily since 2012 (compound annual growth rate, CAGR 2014–2019 = +8 percent) (Figure 7), indicating an increasing interest in the topic.
Between 2002 and 2022, there have been 459 patent families that describe the production of titanium dioxide from ilmenite, and this number is growing rapidly.
Patent activity on titanium metal production has remained quite steady since 2002, indicating a constant but not significantly growing interest in the topic (Figure 16).

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"Patent activity has increased quite steadily since 2012 (compound annual growth rate, CAGR 2014–2019 = +8 percent) (Figure 7), indicating an increasing interest in the topic."},{"excerpt":"Patent activity has increased since 2012, with a CAGR of +11 percent between 2014 and 2019, which can be explained by the growing demand for electric cars."},{"excerpt":"Patent activity has increased strongly since 2012, with a CAGR of +25 percent between 2014 and 2019 (Figure 49)."}]}

examples.excerpt: Patent activity has increased quite steadily since 2012 (compound annual growth rate, CAGR 2014–2019 = +8 percent) (Figure 7), indicating an increasing interest in the topic.
Patent activity has increased since 2012, with a CAGR of +11 percent between 2014 and 2019, which can be explained by the growing demand for electric cars.
Patent activity has increased strongly since 2012, with a CAGR of +25 percent between 2014 and 2019 (Figure 49).

raw_output: {"M":"A mapping f:C×Y→ℕ where for each category C (e.g., speech_voice_music, global_genai, diffusion_model, titanium_ceramics) and each year Y the function f(C,Y) equals the distinct patent‑family count for that category in that year as listed in the CSV table.","T":"A natural‑language summary that describes the temporal trend of the distinct patent‑family counts for a given category, including statements about overall increase, growth rates, notable surges or slow‑downs, and comparisons across years, as reflected by the data in the table."}

parsed_output.M: A mapping f:C×Y→ℕ where for each category C (e.g., speech_voice_music, global_genai, diffusion_model, titanium_ceramics) and each year Y the function f(C,Y) equals the distinct patent‑family count for that category in that year as listed in the CSV table.
parsed_output.T: A natural‑language summary that describes the temporal trend of the distinct patent‑family counts for a given category, including statements about overall increase, growth rates, notable surges or slow‑downs, and comparisons across years, as reflected by the data in the table.

raw_output: {"M":"AverageAnnualGrowthRate = (1/(N-1)) * Σ_{i=1}^{N-1} ((V_{i+1} - V_i) / V_i)","T":"The average annual growth rate of a series of patent‑family counts is calculated by computing the yearly growth rate for each consecutive pair of years (the difference between the later year’s count and the earlier year’s count divided by the earlier year’s count), summing these yearly rates, and dividing by the number of year‑to‑year intervals; the resulting value is expressed as a percentage."}

parsed_output.T: The average annual growth rate of a series of patent‑family counts is calculated by computing the yearly growth rate for each consecutive pair of years (the difference between the later year’s count and the earlier year’s count divided by the earlier year’s count), summing these yearly rates, and dividing by the number of year‑to‑year intervals; the resulting value is expressed as a percentage.

raw_output: {"M":"CAGR = (V_end / V_start)^(1/n) - 1, where V_start is the initial value, V_end is the final value, and n is the number of years between them.","T":"The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) is the average yearly growth rate of a quantity over a period, assuming the growth compounds each year."}

parsed_output.M: CAGR = (V_end / V_start)^(1/n) - 1, where V_start is the initial value, V_end is the final value, and n is the number of years between them.
parsed_output.T: The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) is the average yearly growth rate of a quantity over a period, assuming the growth compounds each year.

raw_output: {"M":"For any category C (e.g., a technology, application area, or geographic region), define a function f_C: Year → ℕ where f_C(y) equals the value in the column \"distinct_<C>_patent_family_count\" for the year y in the provided table.","T":"A time‑series description of the number of distinct patent families associated with a particular category (such as a technology, application, or country) for each year, indicating how many unique patent families were published in that year."}

parsed_output.M: For any category C (e.g., a technology, application area, or geographic region), define a function f_C: Year → ℕ where f_C(y) equals the value in the column "distinct_<C>_patent_family_count" for the year y in the provided table.
parsed_output.T: A time‑series description of the number of distinct patent families associated with a particular category (such as a technology, application, or country) for each year, indicating how many unique patent families were published in that year.

raw_output: {"M":"For each example define a discrete time‑series function f(y) that maps a calendar year y (integer) to the count n of distinct patent families (non‑negative integer) for the described technology or region. The data are given as a set of ordered pairs {(y_i,n_i)}. Trend characteristics can be expressed via first differences Δf(y)=f(y)−f(y−1) or growth rates such as the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) = (f(y2)/f(y1))^{1/(y2−y1)}−1.","T":"Each example provides yearly counts of distinct patent families for a specific technology or geographic market, showing how patent activity changes over time—highlighting periods of rapid growth, slowdown, peaks, or decline."}

parsed_output.M: For each example define a discrete time‑series function f(y) that maps a calendar year y (integer) to the count n of distinct patent families (non‑negative integer) for the described technology or region. The data are given as a set of ordered pairs {(y_i,n_i)}. Trend characteristics can be expressed via first differences Δf(y)=f(y)−f(y−1) or growth rates such as the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) = (f(y2)/f(y1))^{1/(y2−y1)}−1.
parsed_output.T: Each example provides yearly counts of distinct patent families for a specific technology or geographic market, showing how patent activity changes over time—highlighting periods of rapid growth, slowdown, peaks, or decline.

parsed_output.M: Given an ordered list of (year, count) pairs {(y₁,c₁), (y₂,c₂), …, (yₙ,cₙ)} where yᵢ < yᵢ₊₁, define: 1) annual growth rate gᵢ = (cᵢ – cᵢ₋₁) / cᵢ₋₁ for i>1; 2) compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over any interval [y_a, y_b] as CAGR = (c_b / c_a)^(1/(y_b – y_a)) – 1; 3) peak year p = argmax_i cᵢ; 4) trend direction over a window W as the sign of the average gᵢ within that window; and 5) overall change percentage = (c_n – c₁) / c₁ * 100.
parsed_output.T: The data series shows a (increasing/peaking/declining) trend, with the count rising from X in year A to Y in year B, representing a Z% change (or a CAGR of W%). It may highlight a rapid growth phase after year C, a peak in year D, and any subsequent slowdown or decline.

raw_output: {"M":"C(y) = C0 * (1 + g)^{(y - y0)} where C(y) is the distinct patent family count in year y, C0 is the count at a reference year y0 (typically the start of rapid growth, e.g., 2017), and g is the average annual growth rate (approximately 0.45 or 45% for the series shown).","T":"Across the provided examples, the number of distinct patent families for various GenAI categories (e.g., GANs, Japan GenAI, speech/voice/music GenAI, global GenAI) shows a clear upward trend over time, with especially rapid exponential growth after around 2017, corresponding to an average yearly increase of roughly 45%."}

parsed_output.M: C(y) = C0 * (1 + g)^{(y - y0)} where C(y) is the distinct patent family count in year y, C0 is the count at a reference year y0 (typically the start of rapid growth, e.g., 2017), and g is the average annual growth rate (approximately 0.45 or 45% for the series shown).
parsed_output.T: Across the provided examples, the number of distinct patent families for various GenAI categories (e.g., GANs, Japan GenAI, speech/voice/music GenAI, global GenAI) shows a clear upward trend over time, with especially rapid exponential growth after around 2017, corresponding to an average yearly increase of roughly 45%.

raw_output: {"M":"A set of ordered pairs (year, distinct_patent_family_count) representing the annual count of distinct patent families for a specific GenAI category","T":"A chronological series showing the number of distinct patent families published each year for a particular GenAI domain (e.g., GANs, Japan GenAI, speech/voice/music, global GenAI)"}

parsed_output.M: A set of ordered pairs (year, distinct_patent_family_count) representing the annual count of distinct patent families for a specific GenAI category
parsed_output.T: A chronological series showing the number of distinct patent families published each year for a particular GenAI domain (e.g., GANs, Japan GenAI, speech/voice/music, global GenAI)

raw_output: {"M":"A discrete function f:Year→Count defined by the set of ordered pairs {(year_i, count_i)} as given in each example’s table, where each count_i represents the distinct patent family count for that year in the specified GenAI domain or region.","T":"Each example provides a time‑series table that lists, for a particular GenAI category or geographic region, the number of distinct patent families published in each year."}

parsed_output.M: A discrete function f:Year→Count defined by the set of ordered pairs {(year_i, count_i)} as given in each example’s table, where each count_i represents the distinct patent family count for that year in the specified GenAI domain or region.
parsed_output.T: Each example provides a time‑series table that lists, for a particular GenAI category or geographic region, the number of distinct patent families published in each year.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"In terms of patent growth, patent families assigned to GANs show the strongest increase over the past decade. However, patent growth of GAN patent families has slowed down and GAN patent families have increased only moderately over the last three years. A similar slowdown in patent growth can be seen for patent families for VAE models and autoregressive models. In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023."}

parsed_output.excerpt: In terms of patent growth, patent families assigned to GANs show the strongest increase over the past decade. However, patent growth of GAN patent families has slowed down and GAN patent families have increased only moderately over the last three years. A similar slowdown in patent growth can be seen for patent families for VAE models and autoregressive models. In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"In general, patent growth has been high in all applications since 2014. However, over the last three years there have been diverging growth trends with very high growth rates in both smaller application areas such as agriculture and energy management and large fields such as life sciences, security and physical sciences/engineering. In contrast, the number of patent families has stagnated or even declined in certain application areas such as telecommunications, military, arts and humanities or industrial property/law/social and behavioral sciences (Figure 27)."}

parsed_output.excerpt: In general, patent growth has been high in all applications since 2014. However, over the last three years there have been diverging growth trends with very high growth rates in both smaller application areas such as agriculture and energy management and large fields such as life sciences, security and physical sciences/engineering. In contrast, the number of patent families has stagnated or even declined in certain application areas such as telecommunications, military, arts and humanities or industrial property/law/social and behavioral sciences (Figure 27).

raw_output: {"excerpt":"Patents in the use of GenAI in energy management have seen the largest growth, both since 2018 and also in the last three years, with applications in agriculture also growing more in the last three years."}

parsed_output.excerpt: In general, patent growth has been high in all applications since 2014. However, over the last three years there have been diverging growth trends with very high growth rates in both smaller application areas such as agriculture and energy management and large fields such as life sciences, security and physical sciences/engineering. In contrast, the number of patent families has stagnated or even declined in certain application areas such as telecommunications, military, arts and humanities or industrial property/law/social and behavioral sciences (Figure 27).

parsed_output.M: M = { (entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent) | entity ∈ Entities mentioned in the input text, start_year and end_year are the explicit years that define the period over which the CAGR is calculated (or the earliest and latest years referenced for that entity), and cagr_percent is the numeric compound annual growth rate (in percent) reported for that entity in the text }
parsed_output.T: Extract from the paragraph each entity for which a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over a specified period is described, and output a table with columns: entity, start_year, end_year, and cagr_percent.

raw_output: {"M":"CAGR = \\left(\\frac{V_{end}}{V_{start}}\\right)^{\\frac{1}{\\text{end\\_year} - \\text{start\\_year}}} - 1, expressed as a percentage (i.e., multiplied by 100)","T":"The table lists the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of patent‑family publications for each entity (e.g., technology type, country, or application area) over the specified period from start_year to end_year."}

parsed_output.M: CAGR = \left(\frac{V_{end}}{V_{start}}\right)^{\frac{1}{\text{end\_year} - \text{start\_year}}} - 1, expressed as a percentage (i.e., multiplied by 100)
parsed_output.T: The table lists the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of patent‑family publications for each entity (e.g., technology type, country, or application area) over the specified period from start_year to end_year.

raw_output: {"M":"For each entity mentioned in the text, identify the earliest year (start_year) and the latest year (end_year) that delimit the period over which its growth is described, and record the reported compound annual growth rate (CAGR) as a percentage (cagr_percent). Formally, output a tuple (entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent) where cagr_percent = ((V_end/V_start)^(1/(end_year-start_year)) - 1) * 100, with V_start and V_end being the values at the start and end years (the CAGR value is taken directly from the text when provided).","T":"Extract from the narrative each entity for which a growth rate is reported, determine the start and end years of the reported period, and capture the stated compound annual growth rate as a percentage. Present these four fields—entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent—in a compact CSV table."}

parsed_output.M: For each entity mentioned in the text, identify the earliest year (start_year) and the latest year (end_year) that delimit the period over which its growth is described, and record the reported compound annual growth rate (CAGR) as a percentage (cagr_percent). Formally, output a tuple (entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent) where cagr_percent = ((V_end/V_start)^(1/(end_year-start_year)) - 1) * 100, with V_start and V_end being the values at the start and end years (the CAGR value is taken directly from the text when provided).
parsed_output.T: Extract from the narrative each entity for which a growth rate is reported, determine the start and end years of the reported period, and capture the stated compound annual growth rate as a percentage. Present these four fields—entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent—in a compact CSV table.

raw_output: {"M":"CAGR = \\left(\\frac{V_{end}}{V_{start}}\\right)^{\\frac{1}{Y_{end}-Y_{start}}} - 1 \\;\\times 100\\%\\,\\text{, where }V_{start}\\text{ and }V_{end}\\text{ are the numbers of patent families at the start and end years }Y_{start}\\text{ and }Y_{end}.","T":"For each entity (e.g., a technology, country, or application area) the table lists the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of patent‑family publications between the indicated start and end years, expressed as a percentage per year, summarising the observed growth trends described in the accompanying text."}

parsed_output.M: CAGR = \left(\frac{V_{end}}{V_{start}}\right)^{\frac{1}{Y_{end}-Y_{start}}} - 1 \;\times 100\%\,\text{, where }V_{start}\text{ and }V_{end}\text{ are the numbers of patent families at the start and end years }Y_{start}\text{ and }Y_{end}.
parsed_output.T: For each entity (e.g., a technology, country, or application area) the table lists the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of patent‑family publications between the indicated start and end years, expressed as a percentage per year, summarising the observed growth trends described in the accompanying text.

raw_output: {"M":"For each entity mentioned in the input text, identify the start year (Y₁), end year (Y₂), the number of patent families at the start (V₁) and at the end (V₂). Compute the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) as CAGR = ((V₂ / V₁)^(1/(Y₂‑Y₁)) - 1) * 100 and round to one decimal place. Output a table with columns entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent.","T":"Extract each entity’s patent‑family growth period from the paragraph, determine the start and end years and the corresponding patent‑family counts, calculate the CAGR for that period, and present the results as a compact CSV with columns entity, start_year, end_year, and cagr_percent."}

parsed_output.M: For each entity mentioned in the input text, identify the start year (Y₁), end year (Y₂), the number of patent families at the start (V₁) and at the end (V₂). Compute the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) as CAGR = ((V₂ / V₁)^(1/(Y₂‑Y₁)) - 1) * 100 and round to one decimal place. Output a table with columns entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent.
parsed_output.T: Extract each entity’s patent‑family growth period from the paragraph, determine the start and end years and the corresponding patent‑family counts, calculate the CAGR for that period, and present the results as a compact CSV with columns entity, start_year, end_year, and cagr_percent.
